patient_utterances: Hello.

[Clinician asked: "Hi there, it's Doctor Smith from Babylon. Can you confirm your name, date of birth and your address, please?"] Uh,  date of birth twenty second of december, nineteen ninety. Uh, address, uh, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> sixteen  avenue.
[Clinician asked: "OK, that's fine.  Are you in a private place so you can have a consultation?"] Yeah, sure.
[Clinician asked: "What can I do for you today?"] So, um, recently I'm wheezy. Uh, I have feel like kind of short of breath.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and how long has that been going on for?"] Mmm, two, three days. Yeah, quite recently.
[Clinician asked: "And have you ever had similar symptoms in the past?"] Um, I think so. It was a while before, yeah, but I can't remember.
[Clinician asked: "As, was that as an adult or as a child?"] Sorry? Oh, as a , as a adult, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "How long ago was that?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "How long ago was that, that you had similar symptoms?"] I can't remember, sorry.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> And any problems with wheezing when you were a child or any asthma?"] Uh, yes, I had asthma. I also had, uh,  hay fever and  eczema , sorry.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and  when you were a child how bad was your asthma?"] Uh, it's quite bad.
[Clinician asked: "Did you have to take medication?"] Yeah, I take the asthma inhaler.
[Clinician asked: "Do you know which ones you took?"] No, I can't remember.
[Clinician asked: "And did you ever have to go to hospital with it?"] Yeah, sometimes.
[Clinician asked: "Did you ever get severely unwell, have to be on intensive care unit or anything like that?"] No, not that bad but still, sometimes, need to go to hospital to check if it goes well.
[Clinician asked: "Did you have to miss school?"] Uh, yeah, occasionally.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And when do you think it last, when do you think that asthma stopped? At what age did you get to a point where you weren't taking medication anymore?"] Mmm. Few years ago, like, three or four years ago I think? Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> And so this time, how did it start? Did you have any other symptoms? Any chest pain, temperature, cold symptoms, anything like that?"] Yeah, so I have, I have cough and cough a lot, and I have a fever. Um, I also used to walk to work but recently I can't because it's, I, I feel like very short of breath and sometimes I feel like I can't even finish  sentence is quite difficult to, to complete the, the the talking sometimes, yeah. Occasionally.
[Clinician asked: "Right. Have you coughed any phlegm up or anything like that?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "Any, any, um, blood in there when you cough?"] No, I don't think so.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And any chest pain?"] No chest pain, no.
[Clinician asked: "Have you been woken up at night time at all?"] No, just daytime working, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Have you been able to do any exercise?"] No, I can't.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and do you have a peak flow meter at home, where you measure your breathing?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "You have what's called a peak flow meter, which you breathe into to get a score on your breathing?"] No, no.
[Clinician asked: "And do you normally take any asthma inhalers over the last few years?"] Yes, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "You do? So you said you stopped them three to four years ago?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Ohh, um, when I had asthma I used to take this inhaler, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. How about over the last three to four years?"] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Have you taken any medication?"] Um, I took salbutamol? Salbutamol? Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK, so you, you normally take that do you? Or is that just recently?"] Normally, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and how often do you normally need it?"] Every, every few weeks? I'm not sure.
[Clinician asked: "OK. What about when you exercise normally, do you have to use medication? Uh, inhalers?"] Yeah, yeah, a bit.
[Clinician asked: "Pardon?"] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK, um, and do you take any other medications?"] No, nothing else.
[Clinician asked: "Any other medical problems?"] Uh, I have, uh, hay fever as well.
[Clinician asked: "OK, do you take anything for that?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "And any allergies to medications?"] No, no allergies.
[Clinician asked: "Do you smoke?"] No, I don't smoke.
[Clinician asked: "Do you drink any alcohol regularly?"] Yeah, one glass.
[Clinician asked: "How often? Have a glass of what?"] Today, yeah. Uh, one glass of wine.
[Clinician asked: "Every, how often did you say?"] Every two or three days? Depends, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "And who do you live with at home?"] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Who do you live with at home?"] Yeah, .
[Clinician asked: "Who do you live with, do you live on your own, or do you live with"] I live with my partner.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and what's your job, day-to-day?"] Uh, I'm engineer.

[Clinician asked: "And have you been at work in the last few days?"] No I can't because I used to walk <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> walk to work, but recently I, I work from home or sometimes I take sick leave, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and when, at the moment, are you, um, feeling short of breath when you're sitting still? Or just when you walk?"] No. Just walk or sometimes, but currently I feel, I feel well.
[Clinician asked: "Are you able to get  up and down the stairs OK?"] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, and did you say you were finding it hard to complete your sentences?"] Yeah, sometimes.
[Clinician asked: "OK, um."] Yeah, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>, days, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, I mean, it sounds like you've had a flare up of your asthma. It could be, um, triggered by, if there's more pollen around, or there may be, you may have a viral infection, causing it to get worse."] Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm.
[Clinician asked: "Did you say you checked your temperature already?"] Yes, I checked, I have fever <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>.
[Clinician asked: "Was it normal?"] Yeah, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "What was your temperature?"] Uh, thirty seven, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK, that's fine. Um, have you got an asthma inhaler at home?"] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "OK. So what I would suggest is you take it more regularly than you are doing at the moment. Have you got, um, a spacer at home?"] Mm-hmm. Um, do you want to show me how to do it or somehow?
[Clinician asked: "Um, do you have a spacer at home?"] No.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, what I can do is, I can prescribe you a peak flow meter so we can check your breathing level and a spacer to use your inhaler a bit more efficiently, which the pharmacist can show you how you use that when you pick it up."] Yeah, sure.
[Clinician asked: "Um, and if you increase it so you're taking it two to four puffs, like, every four hours?"] Mm-hmm.
[Clinician asked: "Initially, um, if you get a high temperature or your wheezing is getting worse, you need to call us back straightaway so we can just advise you further with that."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "And it will be a good idea when you're feeling better if we can arrange just a review, just to see how your asthma is, because we may need to start some other medication for you."] OK, sounds good, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "You could arrange a review in a week or so, um, but, but before that and if you're, if you're feeling worse."] In a week, right? OK.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and the other thing to do would be to take a regular antihistamine."] Mm-hmm.
[Clinician asked: "Which you can get from the pharmacy, I can put a  on your notes about what to take."] Yeah. Yeah, they'll be helpful.
[Clinician asked: "And then if you, when you get the peak flow meter, if you can take, the pharmacist can show you how to use it, and if you can record a value first thing when you wake up."] Mm-hmm.

[Clinician asked: "And in the evening over the next week, and then we can discuss that over the phone when we look at the figures and see whether you need to go on another inhaler as well."] Oh, awesome, thank you.
[Clinician asked: "Have you got any questions?"] Um, no, that's fine, yeah, thank you very much.
[Clinician asked: "Thanks a lot, bye."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
gold_note:
PC: Wheeze

HPC: 2-3 hx wheeze. No fever, has checked temp - 37. Cold symptoms. Not coughing up sputum, no haemoptysis/CP. Not night time waking. Not exercising at the moment. Sometimes can't complete sentences but no SOB at rest, no problems walking upstairs. More wheezy walking to work. Doesn't have peak flow meter at home. Has missed a few days of work.
Hx asthma as a child, took inhalers ? what. Had hospital admissions and missed school. No ITU. Stopped medication 3-4 years ago but sometimes takes salbutamol still every few weeks. Needs salbutamol when exercises.

PMH Asthma, Hayfever

DH Salbutamol NKDA

SH: Non smoker. 1U alcohol every 2-3 days. Lives with partner. Works as engineer.

Imp: Exacerbation asthma

Plan: Continue to monitor temperature and speak to us if spiking temperatures.
Collect prescription of peak flow meter and spacer. Pharmacist can advise on usage. Record PEFR twice daily, first on waking and at night. Increase salbutamol with spacer to 2-4 puffs 4hrly and arrange a review with us next week. May need to add in another inhaler. If wheeze not settling/getting worse in meantime if feels unwell for urgent appointment.

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
Wheeze
No fever
Not coughing up sputum
Sometimes can't complete sentences but no SOB at rest
Stopped medication 3-4 years ago but sometimes takes salbutamol still every few weeks

naive.response_text:
I understand that you're feeling breathless and uncomfortable, and I want to make sure you get the support you need. Given your history of asthma and current symptoms, including wheezing, shortness of breath, and a fever, it sounds like your asthma might be flaring up. 

To help manage this, I recommend using your asthma inhaler more regularly, as prescribed. I've also arranged for you to receive a peak flow meter and a spacer, which will help you monitor your breathing and use your inhaler more effectively. Additionally, taking a regular antihistamine can help with any allergy-related symptoms you might be experiencing.

Please keep an eye on your symptoms. If your wheezing worsens, your fever rises, or you feel too unwell to manage, don't hesitate to reach out to us. We'll schedule a review in a week to assess your progress and adjust your treatment plan if needed. 

You're not alone in this, and we're here to help you feel better. If you have any questions or concerns, please feel free to ask. Take care and stay well.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thanks for sharing all that with me. It sounds like you're feeling pretty uncomfortable with this wheezing and shortness of breath, especially with the fever on top of it. I can understand how concerning this must be, and I want to make sure we get this under control.

Given your history of asthma and the fact that you're having trouble breathing even at rest, it sounds like this might be an asthma flare-up, possibly triggered by a viral infection or exposure to allergens. The fever adds another layer to consider, so we'll want to keep an eye on that.

For now, I recommend using your asthma inhaler more regularly—two to four puffs every four hours—to help open up your airways. If you don't have a spacer, I can prescribe one to help you use the inhaler more effectively. I'll also suggest taking a regular antihistamine from the pharmacy to help with any allergy symptoms.

We should monitor your breathing with a peak flow meter, which I can arrange for you to pick up. Tracking your breathing can give us valuable information about how your asthma is responding to treatment. If your symptoms get worse, or if you develop a high fever, chest pain, or struggle to catch your breath, please call us right away.

Let's plan to check in within a week to review how you're feeling and adjust your treatment if needed. Before we finish, could you tell me in your own words what we've agreed on? That way, I can make sure everything is clear.
